summary: Gruber recaps C4[1], Jonathan Rentzsch's indie Mac developer conference in Chicago. He covers highlights from multiple sessions including Wil Shipley on marketing, Cabel Sasser's inspiring Panic/Coda talk, Daniel Jalkut on acquiring apps, and several technical presentations. The piece culminates with a critical account of Drunkenbatman's panel discussion, where a provocative claim about racial diversity in the Mac community derailed into an embarrassing, fact-free spectacle. Throughout, Gruber celebrates the indie Mac developer ethos of craft, taste, and attention to detail.
key_points: Wil Shipley's marketing philosophy centers on radical transparency and the principle that apps are nothing more than a collection of tiny details
Cabel Sasser and Panic exemplify Apple-like craft, writing an entire custom toolbar to fix a three-pixel visual discrepancy in Coda
Daniel Jalkut argues that indie developers should think of themselves as business people, and that buying and selling apps is an underutilized strategy
Drunkenbatman's racially provocative panel topic was undermined by hyperbole and a complete absence of supporting data, derailing the discussion
The conference showcased a thriving indie Mac ecosystem where small shops and one-person teams are exerting growing influence
money_quotes: This is all your app is: a collection of tiny details.
Don't announce until it can be downloaded. Don't let it be downloaded until it can be bought.
Just three measly pixels, but there was no other way to make it look just right.
The decision exemplifies what makes Panic, to my eyes, the most Apple-like indie Mac developers.
When opinions run as hot as they can regarding an issue such as racial diversity, it's essential to argue with as many facts as possible.
Much like a train, once a discussion like this falls off the rails, it doesn't come back.
Developers who are good at the former and bored by the latter might do well to sell their creations and move on to the next new app.
There's something riveting about a man driven to forge his own advanced tools.
key_insight: The indie Mac developer community thrives on obsessive attention to craft and detail — but provocative discussions without factual grounding only derail the conversation.
